originalBook: On the Latter Part of Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount, Contained in the Sixth and Seventh Chapters of Matthew.
title: Chapter XIII

source.title: NPNF1-06. St. Augustine: Sermon on the Mount; Harmony of the Gospels; Homilies on the Gospels

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: Rightly, therefore, does he who is intent on cleansing our heart follow up what He has said with a precept, where He says: “Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal: but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.”

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: If, therefore, the heart be on earth, i.e. if one perform anything with a heart bent on obtaining earthly advantage, how will that heart be clean which wallows on earth?

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: But if it be in heaven, it will be clean, because whatever things are heavenly are clean.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For anything becomes polluted when it is mixed with a nature that is inferior, although not polluted of its kind; for gold is polluted even by pure silver, if it be mixed with it: so also our mind becomes polluted by the desire after earthly things, although the earth itself be pure of its kind and order.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: But we would not understand heaven in this passage as anything corporeal, because everything corporeal is to be reckoned as earth.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For he who lays up treasure for himself in heaven ought to despise the whole world.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: Hence it is in that heaven of which it is said, “The heaven of heavens is the Lord’s, i.e. in the spiritual firmament: for it is not in that which is to pass away that we ought to fix and place our treasure and our heart, but in that which ever abideth; but heaven and earth shall pass away.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: And here He makes it manifest that He gives all these precepts with a view to the cleansing of the heart, when He says: “The candle of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: But if thine eye be evil, thy whole body shall be full of darkness.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: If, therefore, the light [lamp] that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!”

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: And this passage we are to understand in such a way as to learn from it that all our works are pure and well-pleasing in the sight of God, when they are done with a single heart, i.e. with a heavenly intent, having that end of love in view; for love is also the fulfilling of the law.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: Hence we ought to take the eye here in the sense of the intent itself, wherewith we do whatever we are doing; and if this be pure and right, and looking at that which ought to be looked at, all our works which we perform in accordance therewith are necessarily good.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: And all those works He has called the whole body; for the apostle also speaks of certain works of which he disapproves as our members, and teaches that they are to be mortified, saying, “Mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth; fornication, uncleanness, covetousness,” and all other such things.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: It is not, therefore, what one does, but the intent with which he does it, that is to be considered.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For this is the light in us, because it is a thing manifest to ourselves that we do with a good intent what we are doing; for everything which is made manifest is light.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For the deeds themselves which go forth from us to human society, have an uncertain issue; and therefore He has called them darkness.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For I do not know, when I present money to a poor man who asks it, either what he is to do with it, or what he is to suffer from it; and it may happen that he does some evil with it, or suffers some evil on account of it, a thing I did not wish to happen when I gave it to him, nor would I have given it with such an intention.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: If, therefore, I did it with a good intention,—a thing which was known to me when I was doing it, and is therefore called light,—my deed also is lighted up, whatever issue it shall have; but that issue, inasmuch as it is uncertain and unknown, is called darkness.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: But if I have done it with a bad intent, the light itself even is darkness.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: For it is spoken of as light, because every one knows with what intent he acts, even when he acts with a bad intent; but the light itself is darkness, because the aim is not directed singly to things above, but is turned downwards to things beneath, and makes, as it were, a shadow by means of a double heart.

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: “If, therefore, the light that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!” i.e., if the very intent of the heart with which you do what you are doing (which is known to you) is polluted by the hunger after earthly and temporal things, and blinded, how much more is the deed itself, whose issue is uncertain, polluted and full of darkness!

verses.book: Our Lord’s Sermon on the Mount.
verses.text: Because, although what you do with an intent which is neither upright nor pure, may turn out for some one’s good, it is the way in which you have done it, not how it has turned out for him, that is reckoned to you.
